speaker: interviewer
text: Survivors of the Shoah-- Visual History Foundation. Today's date is November 1st, 1995. Survivor's name is Jacques Rosenthal. Interviewer is Hilary Helstein. The city is Los Angeles, California, United States of America. The language will be in English. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's November 1st, 1995. And my name is Hilary Helstein, and I am interviewing Jacques Rosenthal in Los Angeles, California, in the United States of America, in the English language. [BACKROUND NOISE] OK. Can you please tell me your full name, first name and last name, and spell it, and if your name was any different at birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacques Rosenthal. J-A-C-Q-U-E-S R-O-S-E-N-T-H-A-L. Before, it was J-A-K-U-B.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born, Jacques?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that time when I was born, it was Russia, in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what, what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: March the 24th, 1911.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Warsaw was part of Russia in 1911?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell me a little bit about your childhood growing up in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, very little I can tell you. I only remember when we came back to Warsaw, end of the-- that was October or November 1917. We came back after the, the revolution. And so I remember only from 18-- from 1918 till '21 till we left Warsaw. In that time, I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the war was still on with Russia and Germany. And the soldier-- we used to live in a street close to the station-- that we saw daily coming wounded soldiers and going back-- new soldiers to the front and my house. In the house that we were living in at that time, there was a bakery. And so they baked bread, and they brought it up to the third floor where we used to live. And my father and mother, who could give a hand, used to throw the bread to the-- to the people that were walking to the station-- soldiers. And then I remember the Declaration-- Balfour Declarations in 1919. We had an uncle that was one of the pioneers in Zionism. It was Nachum Sokolov. In all of the organization for the big event after we had the declaration from Great Britain was a preparation for making our house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in 1919, I was seven, eight years old. They said that I cannot go to the celebration. I didn't say anything. Maybe a few days before they tell me this-- you'll have to stay home.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the day of the celebration, I cried. And Nachum Sokolov was an uncle to my mother. And he asked, why is he crying? And my mother said, he wants to go to the celebration. And he insisted, well, take him. So I remember I went to the celebration was in a big theater in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and I was sitting with all the guests, and the only thing what I still remember from the celebration-- I mean, the start of [INAUDIBLE] played the Hatikvah, I started to cry. Until then, since then, anytime I hear the Hatikvah, I cry. [LAUGHS] So that was end of 1919. And then as my father being originally from Russia, Poland was declared a republic. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father was interned.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my mother was Polish, and we didn't have any rights to be in Warsaw. So we were sent out to the new acquired province from Germany, in the city of Posen. And there, it was like a forced residence. And my mother had permission to go twice-- once a week or twice a week to Warsaw to visit my father in the citadel. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1920, while we were [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] still in Posen, Poznan in Polish, my mother, on one of the trips, she was lost. We didn't hear from her. We didn't know. I didn't know what's going on. But I knew that my mother did. In the mean time, my father was liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he start to do some research. And, and finally she was found in a hospital two weeks later on the border of all-Poland in this new province. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she was hit in the train by the army, the Polish army that was-- came from the United States. They were volunteers. And in the train, there were Jews with beard. So they wanted to kick out, through the windows, the Jews. And my mother stepped in, and she doesn't remember what happened to her. [POINTING] But she was wounded in her head. And she didn't have on her no address, so they took her down to the hospital. And she was not conscious, I suppose, for a week or two. Then finally, she came back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father found her. And the, the promise my father has given to himself-- the first chance he'll have, he'll leave Poland. And that was the end of 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your parents' names while we're talking about them right now? What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, Israel Rosenthal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anna. She was born as Solarska-- Rosenthal.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this where we left, in February the 7th, [BACKROUND NOISE] 1921. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Also, I should pass through the Jewish organization in that time, that we had permission to go to-- in that time-- to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did have permission?

speaker: interviewee
text: We received permission [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as an immigrant. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So going from Germany, my father decided I should go to school in the seminary. And I was in Germany in Berlin from '21 till the mid of '23. And Kaiser Wilhelm's gymnasium was the highest lysee or high school. And from then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were 10 years old at that time, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school in Warsaw prior to going to this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a short time when Poland was declared, there was already a school in Warsaw. So I went to the preschool. Preschool in Europe means-- school in Europe-- lysee or high school is eight years. So before you go into first class, you go to one year for preschool.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that at all, back in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember my teachers. I remember my Hebrew teacher that I had. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I met my Hebrew teacher. He was in Palestine in 1923 and '24.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the other children like in school? Were they all Jewish, or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly it was a Jewish school. It was a private Jewish school. There was no public schools in that time in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your religious background as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had one thing. He used to tell us my duty as a parent is not to teach you, it's to guide you. And when you grow up, you go to school, you'll learn, and then you do anything you want. But my duty as a parent is only to guide you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is-- my father was conservative in that time. He was actively involved. In 1905 [INAUDIBLE] he was involved in-- there was a break after winter. So the Russia in that time lost the war against the Japanese.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the start, it was created in that time, the Polish Socialist Party. But the Polish Socialist Party didn't want to take in no Jews in their membership. And in that time, in that time approximately, they created the Jewish Bund. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's supposed to be the answer to the Polish Socialist Party. And it's supposed to be a democratic way. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so-- and after that, they had difficulty with the government. Russia was still in power. And there were three brothers. And they were all arrested for anti-government activity. And the three brothers-- my father had four brothers. And three brothers was my father, his older brother, and brother between my father and the older brother. And they were all condemned for that time for 15 years to Siberia. But I don't know. By me recollecting, they all three escaped, and my father went to Switzerland. And two brothers remain in Germany-- in 1907, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this all happened prior to your being born.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. Now I'm telling you the activity of my, my father. So there was the Russian government, I suppose, liberated all these prison-- so-called brick prisoners. It was-- they give them pardons. So he declared himself so he could come back, and then he married my, my mother. I think he married my mother in 1908 or 1909. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: As a child living in Warsaw, did you ever have any experiences of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not directly, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of things did you hear, if not directly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Directly-- the school, it was a Jewish school. You were living in a Jewish neighborhood. The only time that we had-- that when I used to go out and play football in that time, and there were children from other schools that didn't mix together. And if it came to a match where we had to play with somebody else, and there were no-- [LAUGHS] there were catholics or any other-- there was always fight. So in that, as a child, you don't take it seriously. But directly, I didn't have any poor experience or bad experience, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have siblings-- brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two sisters only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me-- tell me about them. What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of one sister is Hela, and the other sister is Cypora. Cypora is-- in Yiddish, Cypora is a bird, [YIDDISH]. But her name, Hebrew name, is Cypora.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they older or younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both are younger. I am the oldest in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me your relationship with them growing up. What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: No more relationship for a child. There was no-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no love, no hate [LAUGHS] as a child. Only later, you make the differences. But as a child, it's-- and also, this was part of my parents' education. To them, there was no difference either. I still-- I look back and I still have a feeling to them-- the son, or the daughter, or little daughter-- there was no difference. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit more about your mother now. You tell me a little of your father's background.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's, what's some of your mother's background?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's background-- as I know-- she went to university. In that time, it was already a big problem for a Jew to get into university. But she went to university. She didn't finish university. All in our family, there were always people that spoke many languages. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother spoke different languages-- German, French, and Russian is normal, and Polish. And she was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the occupation. When Germany occupied Poland in that time, she was officially-- the official interpreter to the governor. And the governor was then, in that time, Hindenburg. And she was official interpreter to-- for German and for Russian and for different languages.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the house was spoken-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in that time, as I remember. There was no problem in any language. We heard Hebrew. We heard Yiddish. We heard Russian. And it never created any problem in a children's mind that you don't understand. And it's very hard-- it was very for my parents, I suppose, to have secrets. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you spoke all these languages through her teaching as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke most of the languages, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] including Hebrew, and including German fluently. When I came to the German school, they didn't-- they didn't want to believe that I'm not a German. But I never had any problem for communications.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more about this German school now. This was in 1920-- '21?

speaker: interviewee
text: '21, '22. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was in with a family. And the school was in the heart of the city. And I had to walk but two or three blocks. So the two sisters, they took me to the school. And home, I had to go back home by myself. But a very good relationship-- it was already a mixed school. But it's very hard for average American to understand. There's a big gap between Catholics and Protestant. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The more this country flourished-- the USA flourished-- for 219 years now, not based that you are Catholic, not based that you are Protestant. It was mainly based that the majority was Protestant. And that's why this country flourished, based on Christianity, not Catholics.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me more about Germany now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your-- that year that you were in school in Germany. Your family had gone where now?

speaker: interviewee
text: The family went to Palestine. And I spent two years without seeing my family. The only thing what I still remember about Germany-- that I was when I was hired, my father sent me 3 million marks to pay, to pay for the schooling. And I came the first day to school, and I give the money. And the principal came back to the school to, to me. And he said after, when I finish classes, to come in to see him. He is giving me a letter to my parents. And in the same week, my father sent five million marks more. So-- [LAUGHS] so I was very much impressed. And for five million- eight million marks a month, it was. So that's the only thing what impressed me in that time. But in school, I didn't have any problems. Although, when I came in to school, the first three months, and there were 24 kids in the school. I was number 23. But after three or four months, I went down to number four. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what I remember. Why, why did your parents send you to go to school in Germany while they went off to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have a right education when we came back. Something was missing. And it was, I suppose, it was a new experience for my parents. So let him go to school, and let's see what's going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sisters went with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: One sister was, Hela was with me for, I think, six or eight months. And she didn't qualify, so she went to Palestine. But I finished there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the other children were like, how they behaved, who your friends were? You said it was a mixed school.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, yeah, but it wasn't Catholic. It was Protestant. This is a very important factor. Later on, I realized this. But I, in that time, I didn't know. No hardship, no problems like in Poland. No problems. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1922, you finished your education at this German school.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to Tel Aviv. I spent in Tel Aviv from '22 to '23. In the beginning of '23, my parents decided I should go to Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hard for them-- very hard for them in Palestine in that time, very hard. Financially, whatever they had-- in a short time, he lost everything. And he couldn't even pay the rent in that time. And in Paris, we had a, a, a cousin. She was brought up in our house. And she married somebody in Poland. And he had to go to the military service. And he left. They left for France, and he was in France for a year, and very successfully. So she decided-- my parents decided that she'll take care of me, supposedly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came into France, went to [INAUDIBLE]-- to the school three or four months later. And no problem in France. And how old were you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 13 or 14 years old. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: In France, in the beginning, it was very hard for me. The situation didn't improve in Palestine. And I had to go to work in order to-- and I couldn't stay with my cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't stay after that. I realized that they need a babysitter. And they had two younger children. So they're always, do this and do this. And I didn't any-- so I decided to go on my own. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I had already-- I was already actively involved in the school, and I knew already a little bit the ropes. And I find a job. I was working. I was a porter on a station for three years. I worked at night, from 10:00 at night. And 6 o'clock in the morning, I was already home. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Say something. Just say--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to go to tape number two now.
